Columnar storage: stores each column's values contiguously on disk rather than each row; scanning a single column of a billion-row table reads only that column's data (e.g., 4GB for an integer column) rather than the entire table (e.g., 500GB for a wide row); also enables compression since column values are often similar

Materialized views for OLAP: pre-computed aggregations stored as tables and refreshed periodically; trading storage and freshness for query speed; essential for dashboards that cannot afford to aggregate millions of rows on every page load

Modern data warehouse systems: Amazon Redshift, Google BigQuery, Snowflake, and DuckDB are purpose-built OLAP systems using columnar storage, massively parallel processing, and separate compute and storage for elastic scaling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Design a star schema for an e-commerce analytics warehouse. Create fact_orders (order_id, customer_key, product_key, date_key, store_key, quantity, unit_price, discount, total_amount) and four dimension tables. Load sample data with 1 million order facts. Write analytical queries: (1) total revenue by product category by quarter using GROUP BY with joins across dimensions; (2) top 10 products by revenue with RANK() window function; (3) month-over-month revenue growth using LAG(); (4) revenue subtotals at product, category, and grand total levels using GROUP BY ROLLUP. Compare query performance on the star schema vs the equivalent normalized OLTP schema, showing the star schema is faster for analytics due to fewer joins and pre-joined dimensions.
setup_requirements: PostgreSQL with sample data warehouse (1M+ fact rows)
Star schema ERD reference handout
ROLLUP/CUBE syntax reference
Query performance comparison worksheet

review.value: The star schema deliberately denormalizes dimension tables to minimize joins in analytical queries; a product dimension containing category and department as columns means one join instead of three, trading storage redundancy for query simplicity

An analytical query computing SUM(revenue) on a 1-billion-row table with 100 columns reads only the revenue column (8GB) in a columnar store vs the entire table (800GB) in a row store; this 100x reduction in I/O directly translates to 100x faster queries

GROUP BY ROLLUP(year, quarter, month) produces aggregates at month level, quarter level, year level, and grand total in a single query pass; without ROLLUP, this requires four separate queries UNIONed together

Batch ETL (nightly loads) is simple but data is up to 24 hours stale; real-time ETL (change data capture with streaming) provides fresh data but adds complexity and infrastructure cost. The right choice depends on business requirements for data freshness

class_schedule.topic: Course Review and Database Design Synthesis
learning_objectives: Synthesize knowledge across all topics from relational model through distributed and analytical databases
Trace a complete database project lifecycle: requirements analysis, ER modeling, normalization, SQL implementation, indexing, optimization, security, and operations
Evaluate trade-offs between different database technologies and designs for given requirements
Prepare for the final examination by reviewing key concepts and problem-solving techniques
readings: Silberschatz, Database System Concepts - all chapter summaries
Course lecture notes and assignment solutions
Review sheet distributed in class

lecture_notes.introduction: This final session synthesizes every topic into a unified framework for database system design and operation. A complete database project starts with understanding requirements and ends with a production system that is correct, performant, secure, recoverable, and scalable. We trace this lifecycle from beginning to end. Requirements analysis determines what data the system must manage and what queries it must support. ER modeling captures the conceptual structure. Normalization ensures the logical design is free of anomalies. SQL DDL creates the physical tables with proper constraints and data types. Indexing and query optimization ensure acceptable performance. Transaction design and isolation level selection ensure correctness under concurrency. Backup and recovery strategies ensure durability and disaster recovery. Security measures protect against unauthorized access and SQL injection. Finally, for systems that outgrow a single server, distribution and replication strategies scale capacity and availability. Understanding this complete lifecycle, and how decisions at each stage affect all subsequent stages, is what distinguishes a database engineer from someone who merely writes SQL queries. The course has given you the conceptual tools, practical skills, and theoretical foundations to design, implement, optimize, secure, and operate database systems at professional quality.
key_concepts: Complete lifecycle: requirements -> ER model -> normalization -> SQL DDL -> indexing -> optimization -> transactions -> security -> backup -> distribution
Relational model foundations: relations, keys, integrity constraints, and the mathematical framework that makes query optimization possible
ER modeling to schema: systematic conversion from conceptual design to relational tables preserving all semantic information
Normalization: eliminating redundancy through functional dependency analysis, ensuring data integrity at the schema level
SQL mastery: DDL for schema definition, DML for data manipulation, joins for multi-table queries, window functions for analytics, CTEs for readability
Physical design: B+ tree indexes for range queries, hash indexes for equality, composite indexes for multi-column filters, EXPLAIN for plan analysis
Query optimization: cost-based optimizer, cardinality estimation, join algorithms, predicate pushdown, statistics maintenance
Transaction management: ACID properties, isolation levels, MVCC, deadlock prevention, and the trade-off between consistency and concurrency
Recovery and operations: WAL-based crash recovery, PITR, backup strategies, monitoring, and capacity planning
Beyond relational: NoSQL for specific workloads, distributed databases for scale, data warehouses for analytics, and polyglot persistence for complex systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Full lifecycle walkthrough for a real scenario: design a database for a ride-sharing application. (1) Requirements: riders, drivers, trips, payments, ratings, surge pricing. (2) ER model with entities and relationships. (3) Normalize to 3NF. (4) Create tables in PostgreSQL with constraints. (5) Add indexes for the critical query: find nearby available drivers. (6) Design transaction: atomic trip completion (update trip status, create payment, update driver availability). (7) Security: RLS so riders see only their trips, drivers see only their trips. (8) Discuss scaling: when the system grows to millions of trips per day, shard by city (geographic locality), replicate for read scaling, use Redis for real-time driver location caching.
setup_requirements: Comprehensive review handout covering all topics
Practice problems with solutions for each topic area
Full database lifecycle worksheet
Office hours schedule for pre-exam questions

review.value: Time spent on ER modeling and normalization before writing CREATE TABLE statements always pays off; fixing a schema design error after the application is built requires data migration, code changes, and downtime
Never add an index or denormalize a table without first measuring the performance problem with EXPLAIN ANALYZE; premature optimization creates maintenance burden for speculative benefit

Security (parameterized queries, least privilege, encryption, auditing) must be built in from the start, not added later; retrofitting security is expensive, error-prone, and often triggered by a breach that has already caused damage

review.key: The Right Database for the Job
review.value: PostgreSQL is the right default choice for most applications due to its maturity, feature set, and ecosystem. Consider alternatives only when specific, measured requirements demand it: Redis for sub-millisecond caching, Elasticsearch for full-text search, Neo4j for graph traversal, BigQuery for petabyte analytics

quizzes.topic: ER Modeling, Relational Algebra, and SQL Basics

questions.question: Given a university system with entities Student, Course, and Instructor, where a student can enroll in many courses, each course has exactly one instructor, and an instructor can teach many courses: (a) Draw the ER diagram with cardinality constraints and participation constraints. (b) Convert it to a relational schema, specifying primary keys and foreign keys for each table.
questions.answer:
(a) ER Diagram:
Entities: Student (student_id PK, name, gpa), Course (course_id PK, title, credits), Instructor (instructor_id PK, name, department).
Relationships:
- Enrolls: Student --M:N-- Course (many-to-many, partial participation both sides)
- Teaches: Instructor --1:N-- Course (one instructor teaches many courses, total participation on Course since every course must have an instructor)

(b) Relational Schema:
- Students(student_id PK, name, gpa)
- Instructors(instructor_id PK, name, department)
- Courses(course_id PK, title, credits, instructor_id FK REFERENCES Instructors NOT NULL) -- FK on N-side for 1:N, NOT NULL for total participation
- Enrollments(student_id FK REFERENCES Students, course_id FK REFERENCES Courses, PRIMARY KEY(student_id, course_id), enrollment_date, grade) -- junction table for M:N

rubric.key: Correct ER diagram with proper cardinality notation (M:N for enrolls, 1:N for teaches)
Correct relational schema with junction table for M:N and FK on N-side for 1:N
Total participation of Course in Teaches relationship enforced with NOT NULL FK

questions.question: Using relational algebra, express: 'Find the names of students who are enrolled in the course with course_id CS301 but are NOT enrolled in the course with course_id CS302.' Tables: Students(student_id, name, gpa), Enrollments(student_id, course_id, grade).
questions.answer:
Step 1: Students enrolled in CS301:
CS301_students = pi_{student_id}(sigma_{course_id='CS301'}(Enrollments))

Step 2: Students enrolled in CS302:
CS302_students = pi_{student_id}(sigma_{course_id='CS302'}(Enrollments))

Step 3: Students in CS301 but not CS302:
result_ids = CS301_students - CS302_students

Step 4: Get their names:
result = pi_{name}(result_ids join Students)

Complete expression:
pi_{name}((pi_{student_id}(sigma_{course_id='CS301'}(Enrollments)) - pi_{student_id}(sigma_{course_id='CS302'}(Enrollments))) join Students)

rubric.key: Correct selection and projection for each course's student set
Correct use of set difference to find CS301-only students
Correct final join with Students to retrieve names

questions.question: Write a SQL query that returns each department's name and the number of students with GPA above 3.0, but only for departments with more than 10 such students. Sort by count descending. Tables: Students(student_id, name, gpa, department_id), Departments(department_id, department_name).
questions.answer:
SELECT d.department_name, COUNT(*) AS high_gpa_count
FROM Students s
JOIN Departments d ON s.department_id = d.department_id
WHERE s.gpa > 3.0
GROUP BY d.department_id, d.department_name
HAVING COUNT(*) > 10
ORDER BY high_gpa_count DESC;

rubric.key: Correct JOIN between Students and Departments
WHERE clause filters by GPA before grouping (not in HAVING)
Correct GROUP BY, HAVING, and ORDER BY
Correct use of COUNT and column aliasing

questions.question: Given tables: Employees(emp_id, name, dept_id, salary), Departments(dept_id, dept_name, manager_id FK -> Employees). Write a single SQL query that returns each department's name, the manager's name, the department's average salary, and a ranking of departments by average salary (highest first). Use window functions for the ranking.
questions.answer:
SELECT 
    d.dept_name,
    m.name AS manager_name,
    AVG(e.salary) AS avg_salary,
    RANK() OVER (ORDER BY AVG(e.salary) DESC) AS dept_rank
FROM Departments d
JOIN Employees m ON d.manager_id = m.emp_id
JOIN Employees e ON e.dept_id = d.dept_id
GROUP BY d.dept_id, d.dept_name, m.name
ORDER BY dept_rank;

rubric.key: Correct self-join pattern: Employees as both managers and department members
Correct GROUP BY with AVG aggregate for department salary
Correct RANK() window function over aggregated AVG

questions.question: A table OrderDetails has attributes: {order_id, product_id, product_name, product_category, category_discount_pct, quantity, unit_price}. Functional dependencies: order_id, product_id -> quantity, unit_price; product_id -> product_name, product_category; product_category -> category_discount_pct. (a) What is the highest normal form of this table? (b) Decompose to BCNF, showing each step.
questions.answer:
(a) Candidate key: {order_id, product_id} (its closure includes all attributes via the given FDs).

Check 1NF: all attributes are atomic. Yes, in 1NF.
Check 2NF: product_id -> product_name, product_category is a partial dependency (product_id is part of the composite key). NOT in 2NF.

Highest normal form: 1NF.

(b) BCNF Decomposition:
Step 1: Violation: product_id -> product_name, product_category (product_id is not a superkey).
Decompose: Products(product_id, product_name, product_category) and OrderDetails1(order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price, category_discount_pct).

Step 2: In OrderDetails1, check FDs. product_category is gone... wait, we need to check if category_discount_pct depends on something. product_category -> category_discount_pct, but product_category is in Products, not OrderDetails1. Actually, via product_id -> product_category -> category_discount_pct, we have a transitive dependency. But category_discount_pct is still in OrderDetails1 and depends on product_category which is no longer in OrderDetails1.

Let me redo: product_id -> product_name, product_category AND product_category -> category_discount_pct.

Step 1: Decompose on product_category -> category_discount_pct:
Categories(product_category, category_discount_pct) and Remainder(order_id, product_id, product_name, product_category, quantity, unit_price).

Step 2: In Remainder, product_id -> product_name, product_category. product_id is not a superkey (key is {order_id, product_id}).
Decompose: Products(product_id, product_name, product_category) and OrderItems(order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price).

Final BCNF tables:
- Categories(product_category PK, category_discount_pct)
- Products(product_id PK, product_name, product_category FK)
- OrderItems(order_id, product_id, quantity, unit_price, PK(order_id, product_id))

All three tables are in BCNF: in each, every non-trivial FD has a superkey as determinant.

rubric.key: Correct identification of 1NF (not 2NF due to partial dependency)
Correct identification of all FD violations of BCNF
Correct final decomposition into three BCNF tables with proper keys and FKs

questions.question: Explain why this query is incorrect for finding 'customers who have ordered every product in the Electronics category,' and write the correct version. Incorrect: SELECT c.name FROM Customers c JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id JOIN Products p ON o.product_id = p.id WHERE p.category = 'Electronics' GROUP BY c.id, c.name HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) > 0;
questions.answer:
The incorrect query finds customers who have ordered AT LEAST ONE electronics product (COUNT > 0), not customers who have ordered EVERY electronics product. It would need to compare the count of distinct products ordered against the total count of electronics products.

Correct query:
SELECT c.name
FROM Customers c
JOIN Orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
JOIN Products p ON o.product_id = p.id
WHERE p.category = 'Electronics'
GROUP BY c.id, c.name
HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT p.id) = (
    SELECT COUNT(*) FROM Products WHERE category = 'Electronics'
);

The HAVING clause now checks that the customer has ordered a number of distinct electronics products equal to the total number of electronics products, implementing the 'for all' (division) semantics.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies the error: COUNT > 0 means 'at least one' not 'every'
Correct fix using COUNT(DISTINCT) = (SELECT COUNT(*))
Explains the 'for all' / relational division semantics

questions.question: A B+ tree index has order (max children) of 4, meaning internal nodes can hold up to 3 keys and 4 pointers, and leaf nodes can hold up to 3 key-pointer pairs. Starting from an empty tree, show the B+ tree structure after inserting keys: 10, 20, 5, 15, 25, 30, 12. Show any node splits that occur.
questions.answer:
Insert 10: Root leaf: [10]
Insert 20: Root leaf: [10, 20]
Insert 5: Root leaf: [5, 10, 20] (full)
Insert 15: Leaf overflow, split:
  Left leaf: [5, 10], Right leaf: [15, 20]
  New root internal node: [15] with pointers to left and right leaves.
  Tree:
       [15]
      /    \
  [5,10] [15,20]

Insert 25: Goes to right leaf: [15, 20, 25] (full but no overflow yet)
Insert 30: Right leaf overflow [15,20,25,30], split:
  Left leaf: [15, 20], Right leaf: [25, 30]
  Promote 25 to root: Root becomes [15, 25]
  Tree:
       [15, 25]
      /    |    \
  [5,10] [15,20] [25,30]

Insert 12: Goes to left leaf: [5, 10, 12] (fits, no split needed)
Final tree:
       [15, 25]
      /    |    \
 [5,10,12] [15,20] [25,30]

Leaf nodes are linked left-to-right for range scans.

rubric.key: Correct insertions in sorted order within leaves
Correct split when leaf overflows (split at midpoint, promote to parent)
Correct final tree structure with proper keys in internal and leaf nodes

questions.question:
Given this EXPLAIN ANALYZE output for a query, identify two performance problems and propose specific fixes:

Sort (cost=15234.12..15289.45 rows=22134 width=64) (actual time=850.234..862.112 rows=22134)
  Sort Key: o.order_date
  Sort Method: external merge Disk: 1832kB
  -> Hash Join (cost=3456.00..12345.67 rows=22134 width=64) (actual time=245.678..678.901 rows=22134)
        Hash Cond: (o.customer_id = c.customer_id)
        -> Seq Scan on orders o (cost=0.00..8123.45 rows=22134 width=40) (actual time=0.012..423.456 rows=22134)
              Filter: (status = 'shipped' AND order_date > '2024-01-01')
              Rows Removed by Filter: 477866
        -> Hash (cost=2345.00..2345.00 rows=5000 width=32) (actual time=23.456..23.456 rows=5000)
              -> Seq Scan on customers c (cost=0.00..2345.00 rows=5000 width=32) (actual time=0.008..18.234 rows=5000)
questions.answer:
Problem 1: Sequential Scan on orders with heavy filtering. The orders table has 500,000 rows (22,134 + 477,866 removed), but only 22,134 match the filter. The seq scan reads ALL 500,000 rows. Fix: CREATE INDEX idx_orders_status_date ON orders(status, order_date) WHERE status = 'shipped'; This partial index on shipped orders with order_date allows an index scan that reads only qualifying rows. The status equality should come first in the composite index.

Problem 2: Sort spilling to disk. The sort is using 'external merge Disk: 1832kB', meaning it exceeded work_mem and spilled to disk, which is much slower than an in-memory sort. Two fixes: (a) Increase work_mem (SET work_mem = '4MB') to allow in-memory sorting, or (b) extend the index to include order_date for ordering: if the index provides rows in order_date order, the explicit sort is eliminated entirely. With the index idx_orders_status_date ON orders(status, order_date), rows come out already sorted by order_date for status='shipped', eliminating the sort node.

rubric.key: Identifies sequential scan on orders as problem with correct row count analysis
Proposes correct composite or partial index to eliminate seq scan
Identifies disk-based sort as problem
Proposes either work_mem increase or index-based sort elimination

questions.answer:
Under READ COMMITTED:
- T1's first SELECT returns 1000 (current committed value).
- T2 updates balance to 800 and commits.
- T1's second SELECT returns 800 (T2 has committed, and READ COMMITTED sees latest committed data for each statement). This is a non-repeatable read.
- T1's UPDATE: balance = 800 + 500 = 1300. (The UPDATE reads the latest committed value, which is 800).
- Final balance: 1300.

Under REPEATABLE READ:
- T1's first SELECT returns 1000 (snapshot taken at transaction start).
- T2 updates to 800 and commits.
- T1's second SELECT returns 1000 (REPEATABLE READ sees the snapshot from transaction start, so the same value as before -- no non-repeatable read).
- T1's UPDATE: In PostgreSQL's REPEATABLE READ implementation, the UPDATE would detect that row id=1 was modified by a committed concurrent transaction since T1's snapshot. PostgreSQL would raise a serialization error: 'could not serialize access due to concurrent update.' T1 must retry.
- If T1 retries, it gets a new snapshot where balance = 800, reads 800, adds 500, final balance = 1300.

Key difference: READ COMMITTED allows T1 to proceed with potentially surprising values; REPEATABLE READ detects the conflict and forces a retry, providing stronger consistency at the cost of potential transaction aborts.

rubric.key: Correct READ COMMITTED behavior: second SELECT returns 800 (non-repeatable read)
Correct final balance under READ COMMITTED (1300)
Correct REPEATABLE READ behavior: serialization error on UPDATE due to concurrent modification

questions.question:
A database system uses WAL with the following log (LSN, Transaction, Operation, Before, After):
1. T1, UPDATE A, 100, 150
2. T2, UPDATE B, 200, 250
3. T1, UPDATE C, 300, 350
4. CHECKPOINT
5. T2, UPDATE D, 400, 450
6. T1, COMMIT
7. T3, UPDATE A, 150, 175

The system crashes after log record 7. Which transactions need redo? Which need undo? What is the final value of each data item (A, B, C, D)?
questions.answer:
At crash time:
- T1: COMMITTED (log record 6). Needs REDO to ensure all changes are on disk.
- T2: No commit record. Needs UNDO to reverse uncommitted changes.
- T3: No commit record. Needs UNDO to reverse uncommitted changes.

Recovery process (ARIES-style):
Analysis phase: Start from checkpoint (record 4). Active transactions at checkpoint: T1, T2. Scan forward: T2 updates D (record 5), T1 commits (record 6), T3 updates A (record 7). At crash: active = {T2, T3}.

Redo phase (forward from checkpoint): Replay ALL logged changes to restore pre-crash state:
- Record 5: D = 450 (T2's update)
- Record 6: T1 commit (no data change)
- Record 7: A = 175 (T3's update)
Note: Records 1-3 are before checkpoint, but we also redo them if not yet flushed: A=150, B=250, C=350.

Undo phase (backward from crash): Undo uncommitted transactions:
- Record 7: T3 uncommitted, undo: A = 150 (restore before-image)
- Record 5: T2 uncommitted, undo: D = 400 (restore before-image)
- Record 2: T2 uncommitted, undo: B = 200 (restore before-image)

Final values:
A = 150 (T1 committed 100->150, T3 undone 175->150)
B = 200 (T2 undone 250->200)
C = 350 (T1 committed 300->350)
D = 400 (T2 undone 450->400)

rubric.key: Correct identification of which transactions need redo (T1) and undo (T2, T3)
Correct redo phase: all changes replayed forward from checkpoint
Correct undo phase: uncommitted changes reversed using before-images
Correct final values: A=150, B=200, C=350, D=400

questions.question: A social media application needs to store user profiles, posts, and friendships. The system must support: (1) view a user's profile with their last 20 posts (latency < 10ms), (2) view a user's news feed (posts from friends, sorted by time), (3) full-text search on posts. Recommend a database architecture (which database technology for each use case) and justify each choice.
questions.answer:
Recommended polyglot persistence architecture:

1. User profiles + posts: MongoDB (document database).
Justification: User profiles have varying fields (some users have bio, some have employer, etc.), making document model a natural fit. Embedding the last 20 posts within the user document allows a single document read for the profile page, achieving < 10ms latency. Posts beyond the last 20 can be in a separate posts collection indexed by user_id and timestamp.

2. News feed: Redis (sorted sets) + pre-computation.
Justification: Reading a user's news feed requires collecting posts from all friends and sorting by time. Doing this on-the-fly via JOINs or aggregation is too slow at scale. Instead, use a fan-out-on-write approach: when a user posts, push the post ID into each friend's news feed sorted set in Redis (ZADD by timestamp). Reading the feed is then a single ZREVRANGE command (O(log N + K) for K items), easily under 10ms.

3. Full-text search on posts: Elasticsearch.
Justification: Full-text search requires inverted indexes, tokenization, stemming, relevance scoring, and fuzzy matching. Elasticsearch is purpose-built for this, providing sub-second full-text search across billions of documents. Neither MongoDB's text search nor PostgreSQL's tsvector match Elasticsearch's performance and relevance tuning capabilities at scale.

4. Friendship graph and friend recommendations: Neo4j (graph database) or PostgreSQL.
Justification: 'Friends of friends' queries (for recommendations) require multi-hop graph traversals that are O(n^k) with JOINs but O(k) per traversal in a graph database. For moderate scale, PostgreSQL with recursive CTEs works; at large scale, Neo4j is more efficient.

Overall: MongoDB as the primary data store, Redis for real-time feeds and caching, Elasticsearch for search, with PostgreSQL for transactional operations (payments, account management) requiring ACID guarantees.

rubric.key: Appropriate technology choice for each use case with correct justification
Understanding of trade-offs: why not use one database for everything
Practical architecture that addresses all three requirements within latency constraints

questions.question: In a distributed database sharded by customer_id, explain why the query SELECT product_id, SUM(quantity) FROM orders GROUP BY product_id is expensive, and describe two strategies to make it efficient.
questions.answer:
The query is expensive because it groups by product_id, which is NOT the shard key. Since the data is sharded by customer_id, orders for the same product are scattered across ALL shards. The query must:
1. Send the query to every shard.
2. Each shard computes a partial GROUP BY for the products in its subset of orders.
3. A coordinator node collects all partial results and merges them, re-aggregating groups that span multiple shards.

This requires network transfer of intermediate results from every shard, and the coordinator may become a bottleneck.

Strategy 1: Materialized summary table. Maintain a pre-aggregated table product_order_summary(product_id, total_quantity) that is updated incrementally on each order insert (or refreshed periodically via batch job). The summary table can be replicated to all nodes or kept on a single analytics node. Queries against the summary table avoid the cross-shard aggregation entirely.

Strategy 2: Parallel partial aggregation with two-phase approach. Each shard computes its local partial aggregation (product_id, local_sum) and sends only the aggregated results (not raw rows) to the coordinator. The coordinator merges by product_id, summing the local_sums. This reduces network transfer from millions of raw order rows to at most (number_of_products * number_of_shards) partial aggregation rows. This is how most distributed SQL engines (Presto, Spark SQL, CockroachDB) handle cross-shard aggregations.

rubric.key: Correctly explains why grouping by non-shard-key requires cross-shard coordination
First strategy is practical and correctly described (materialized view or summary table)
Second strategy is practical and correctly described (partial aggregation or data redistribution)

quizzes.topic: Database Security, Data Warehousing, and System Design

questions.answer:
Vulnerability: SQL injection. The username parameter is directly interpolated into the SQL string without sanitization or parameterization.

Exploit: If username = "' OR '1'='1' --", the query becomes:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = '' OR '1'='1' --'
This returns ALL users because '1'='1' is always true, and the -- comments out the trailing quote.

More dangerous exploit: username = "'; DROP TABLE users; --" would execute:
SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = ''; DROP TABLE users; --'
This drops the entire users table.

Corrected version:
def search_users(conn, username):
    query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = %s"
    cursor = conn.execute(query, (username,))
    return cursor.fetchall()

The corrected version uses a parameterized query where %s is a placeholder. The database driver sends the query structure and the parameter value separately, ensuring the parameter is always treated as a literal string value, never as SQL code, regardless of its content.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies SQL injection vulnerability from string interpolation
Provides a working exploit demonstrating data leakage or destruction
Correct fix using parameterized query with proper placeholder syntax

questions.question: Design a star schema for an airline analytics data warehouse. The fact table should support queries like: 'total revenue by route by month', 'average delay by airline by airport', and 'passenger count by fare class by quarter'. Specify the fact table with measures and foreign keys, and at least four dimension tables with key attributes.
questions.answer:
Fact table: fact_flights
- flight_key (surrogate PK)
- date_key FK -> dim_date
- route_key FK -> dim_route
- airline_key FK -> dim_airline
- aircraft_key FK -> dim_aircraft
- Measures: passenger_count, revenue, departure_delay_minutes, arrival_delay_minutes, fare_class_revenue_economy, fare_class_revenue_business, fare_class_revenue_first, flight_duration_minutes

Dimension tables:

1. dim_date: date_key PK, full_date, day_of_week, month, quarter, year, is_holiday, season

2. dim_route: route_key PK, origin_airport_code, origin_city, origin_country, destination_airport_code, destination_city, destination_country, distance_miles, route_category (domestic/international)

3. dim_airline: airline_key PK, airline_code, airline_name, alliance_name, hub_airports, country_of_origin

4. dim_aircraft: aircraft_key PK, aircraft_model, manufacturer, seat_capacity_economy, seat_capacity_business, seat_capacity_first, range_miles, aircraft_age_years

Query support:
- 'Total revenue by route by month': SELECT r.origin_city, r.destination_city, d.month, d.year, SUM(f.revenue) FROM fact_flights f JOIN dim_route r ... JOIN dim_date d ... GROUP BY ...
- 'Average delay by airline by airport': SELECT a.airline_name, r.origin_airport_code, AVG(f.departure_delay_minutes) ... GROUP BY ...
- 'Passenger count by fare class by quarter': SELECT d.quarter, SUM(f.passenger_count), SUM(f.fare_class_revenue_economy), ... GROUP BY d.quarter

rubric.key: Fact table with appropriate measures (revenue, delay, passengers) and foreign keys
Four well-designed dimension tables with relevant descriptive attributes
Schema correctly supports all three specified query patterns

questions.question: A database team is debating whether to normalize or denormalize a reporting table. The table stores order data with customer information repeated on every order row. The system has 10 million orders, 100,000 customers, and the reporting query (joining orders with customers) runs 1,000 times per day, while order inserts happen 50,000 times per day. Argue both sides and make a recommendation.
questions.answer:
Argument for normalization (separate orders and customers tables):
- Eliminates redundancy: customer name, email, address stored once (100K rows) instead of repeated across 10M order rows. Saves significant storage.
- Update consistency: changing a customer's address updates one row instead of thousands of order rows. No risk of partial updates creating inconsistent data.
- Write performance: 50,000 inserts/day only write order data, not redundant customer data; each insert is smaller.
- Data integrity: no possibility of orders referencing different versions of customer data.

Argument for denormalization (combined table):
- Read performance: 1,000 reporting queries/day avoid the JOIN entirely, reading from a single table. With 10M rows and proper indexes, this can be significantly faster than joining 10M orders with 100K customers.
- Simpler queries: no JOIN syntax, no risk of incorrect join conditions.
- Historical accuracy: if a customer changes their address, old orders retain the address at the time of the order (though this can also be achieved with a slowly-changing dimension approach).

Recommendation: NORMALIZE the operational database (orders + customers) and create a materialized view or data warehouse table with the denormalized view for reporting. This gets the best of both worlds: clean operational data with referential integrity for the 50,000 daily writes, and a pre-joined denormalized table for the 1,000 daily reporting reads. Refresh the materialized view nightly or hourly depending on reporting freshness requirements.

This approach is justified because the write-to-read ratio is 50:1 (writes dominate), the storage savings from normalization are substantial (100x fewer customer data copies), and the materialized view eliminates the JOIN cost for reads without sacrificing operational data integrity.

rubric.key: Valid arguments for both normalization and denormalization with specific trade-offs
Practical recommendation with clear justification based on workload characteristics
Mention of materialized view or hybrid approach as best-of-both-worlds solution

major_assignments.title: Database Design and Implementation: E-Commerce Platform
major_assignments.description: Design and implement a complete database for an e-commerce platform. Start from business requirements, produce an ER diagram, normalize to BCNF, implement in PostgreSQL with comprehensive constraints and indexes, populate with realistic data, and write a suite of complex queries demonstrating advanced SQL proficiency.
objectives: Apply the full database design lifecycle from requirements analysis through physical implementation
Produce a normalized schema in BCNF with proper constraints, referential integrity, and appropriate data types

Optimize query performance using indexes, EXPLAIN ANALYZE, and query rewriting

Professional writing quality throughout all deliverables

major_assignments.title: Transaction Processing and Recovery System Analysis
major_assignments.description: Build a concurrent transaction workload simulator that tests database isolation levels, demonstrates concurrency anomalies, measures performance under different isolation and locking configurations, and implements a WAL-based recovery scenario analysis. This project bridges theory and practice by making abstract concurrency concepts tangible through measurement.
objectives: Implement a multi-threaded workload generator that simulates concurrent bank transfer transactions against PostgreSQL
Demonstrate and measure concurrency anomalies (dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, lost updates) under different isolation levels
Measure throughput (transactions per second) and latency under READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, and SERIALIZABLE
Analyze WAL log records for a sequence of transactions and simulate crash recovery scenarios on paper
deliverables: Workload simulator source code (Python or Java) with configurable thread count, isolation level, and transaction mix
Anomaly demonstration: test cases that reliably trigger each anomaly type under permissive isolation and prove prevention under stricter isolation
Performance benchmark report: throughput and latency graphs across isolation levels with 1, 2, 4, 8, and 16 concurrent threads
WAL recovery analysis (3 pages): given a provided transaction log, trace the ARIES recovery algorithm (analysis, redo, undo) showing the final database state

criteria: Workload simulator correctly connects to PostgreSQL with configurable isolation levels
Multi-threaded execution with proper connection pooling (each thread gets its own connection and transaction)
Anomaly demonstrations are reproducible: test cases trigger anomalies reliably and prevention is verified
No race conditions in the simulator itself (measurement code is thread-safe)
Throughput and latency graphs are clear with proper axes, labels, error bars, and legends
Anomaly demonstrations include before/after screenshots or log output showing the anomaly and prevention
WAL recovery trace is presented step-by-step with clear state tables
All figures and tables are publication quality
Benchmark methodology is sound: proper warm-up period, multiple runs, statistical summary
Performance differences between isolation levels are measured and explained in terms of lock contention and MVCC overhead
Deadlock rate is measured under SERIALIZABLE and retry strategy is implemented and documented
Trade-off analysis connects measured performance to theoretical understanding of concurrency control mechanisms
Benchmark report explains methodology, environment, and measurement procedures clearly enough for reproduction
Anomaly demonstrations include theoretical explanation of why each anomaly occurs and why the isolation level prevents it
WAL recovery analysis correctly applies ARIES algorithm with explicit analysis, redo, and undo phases
Professional writing quality throughout all deliverables
Working multi-threaded transaction workload simulator with configurable isolation
Reproducible anomaly demonstrations with theoretical explanation
Rigorous performance benchmarks comparing isolation levels under varying concurrency
Correct WAL recovery analysis applying ARIES algorithm

major_assignments.title: Polyglot Database Architecture: Design and Evaluation
major_assignments.description: Design, implement, and evaluate a polyglot persistence architecture for a real-time analytics application. Implement the same application using PostgreSQL alone and then using a combination of PostgreSQL, Redis, and MongoDB. Compare performance, development complexity, and operational characteristics. This project teaches practical database technology selection and the trade-offs of polyglot persistence.
objectives: Design a data model for a real-time dashboard application in both relational and polyglot architectures
Implement the relational version in PostgreSQL with proper schema design and indexing
Implement the polyglot version using PostgreSQL for transactional data, Redis for caching and real-time counters, and MongoDB for flexible document storage
Benchmark both architectures on identical workloads and analyze the trade-offs
deliverables: Architecture design document (3 pages) describing both approaches with data flow diagrams
PostgreSQL-only implementation: schema, indexes, application code, and sample queries
Polyglot implementation: PostgreSQL schema, Redis data structures, MongoDB collections, and application code orchestrating all three
Benchmark report (3-4 pages): latency comparison for dashboard queries, throughput comparison for data ingestion, cache hit rate analysis, and operational complexity assessment

criteria: PostgreSQL schema is properly normalized with appropriate indexes for the dashboard queries
Redis usage is appropriate: caching frequently-accessed data, sorted sets for leaderboards, pub/sub for real-time updates
MongoDB document model correctly represents flexible data (user preferences, event metadata with varying fields)
Application code correctly orchestrates reads and writes across all three databases with proper error handling
Architecture diagrams clearly show data flow between components
Benchmark results are presented in clear graphs comparing both architectures
Redis and MongoDB data structure choices are visualized
All deliverables are professionally formatted
Benchmarks measure realistic workloads: mixed read-write, varying concurrency levels, dashboard query latency
Cache hit rate analysis demonstrates Redis effectiveness with measured latency reduction
Operational complexity is honestly assessed: deployment, monitoring, backup, failure modes for polyglot vs single-DB
Recommendations are data-driven: when the complexity of polyglot persistence is justified and when PostgreSQL alone is sufficient
Architecture document explains each technology choice with justification from CAP theorem and workload analysis
Benchmark methodology is reproducible with clear environment description and measurement procedures
Trade-off analysis is balanced, acknowledging both advantages and disadvantages of each approach
Professional writing quality throughout all deliverables
Working implementations in both PostgreSQL-only and polyglot architectures
Appropriate technology selection with clear justification for each component
Rigorous benchmarks demonstrating measurable trade-offs
Balanced analysis with data-driven recommendations

course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of SQL approaches is encouraged. All queries, schemas, and written analysis must be individual work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying SQL queries from the internet or other students without attribution constitutes plagiarism.
